title: Getters & Setters 🚪
description: The proper way to interact with private data.

slides.title: The Trapped Data 📦
slides.content:
If we make `private int age;`, our object's data is safe!

...But it's too safe. Now we can't even read the dog's age to print it out on the screen.

We need to build controlled 'Doors' to let people interact with the private data.

slides.title: Getters 📤
slides.content:
A **Getter** is a `public` method whose only job is to `return` the value of a `private` variable.

It allows the outside world to READ the data, without giving them permission to CHANGE the data.

quizData.question: What is the industry-standard naming convention for a method that reads a variable named `score`?
quizData.explanation: Always prefix the capitalized variable name with the word `get`. e.g., getHealth(), getName().

slides.title: Setters 📥
slides.content:
A **Setter** is a `public` method whose job is to intelligently update a private variable.

Why use a Setter instead of just making the variable public? Because Setters let you add **Safety Checks**!

slides.title: Encapsulation 🛡️
slides.content:
## Core OOP Concept: Encapsulation

The practice of wrapping up your private variables and securing them with public Getter and Setter methods is called **Encapsulation**.

It acts as a shield, ensuring your object's internal state remains valid and untampered.
